Explain the self-reinforcing cycle between installed base and complementary goods in dominant design competition.	Large installed base → more complementary goods developed → more users attracted → larger installed base (positive feedback loop)This network effect dynamic means that even a technically inferior technology can win if it gets ahead in installed base and complements early.Example: Windows PCs attracted more software developers than OS/2, which attracted more users, which attracted more developers — a self-reinforcing cycle that made Windows dominant.
What is path dependency in the context of dominant designs, and why does it matter strategically?	Path dependency means end results depend greatly on the historical events leading up to the outcome — early choices shape which technology wins, not just technical meritThis means technologically superior products do not always win. Winner-take-all markets require different firm strategies than markets with less pressure for a single dominant design.Example: VHS beat Betamax despite Betamax having superior video quality, because VHS gained an early installed base advantage.
What are the four strategic steps for 'fighting standards wars' to win dominant design competition?	1) Determine potential for standards emergence (analyze network externalities), 2) Assemble allies (partners, complementors, competitors), 3) Pre-empt the market (enter early, attract key customers, use penetration pricing), 4) Manage expectations (use pre-launch publicity to convince market you will win)Standards wars are won through coalition-building and momentum, not just technology.Example: Blu-ray defeated HD-DVD partly by assembling studio allies and managing consumer expectations about the winning standard.
What two dimensions of value determine a technology's overall attractiveness in increasing-returns industries, and why can a technically superior product lose?	1) Standalone value (functionality, ease of use, aesthetics) and 2) Network externality value (installed base size, availability of complementary goods)A new technology with more standalone functionality may offer less overall value if it has a smaller installed base or poor complement availability.Example: NeXT Computers were technically advanced but could not compete with the installed base value and complementary good value of Windows-based PCs.
What are the four strategies for sustaining a dominant standard once won?	1) Don't fall behind on technology, 2) Ensure backward compatibility, 3) Offer customers a migration path to address disruptive technology threats, 4) Reinforce the standard with other resources (e.g., brand)Sustaining a standard requires ongoing investment — complacency invites disruption.Example: Microsoft maintained Windows dominance partly through backward compatibility, ensuring users could upgrade without losing existing software investments.
What is the difference between 'competence-enhancing' and 'competence-destroying' innovation? Give an example of each.	Competence-enhancing: builds on the firm's existing knowledge base. Competence-destroying: renders a firm's existing competencies obsolete.Whether an innovation is competence-enhancing or -destroying depends on the perspective of a particular firm — the same innovation can be enhancing for one and destroying for another.Example: Intel's Pentium 4 (enhancing — built on Pentium III). Electronic calculators (destroying for Keuffel & Esser — rendered their slide rule expertise obsolete).
What is the difference between 'architectural' and 'component' (modular) innovation? Give an example of each.	Component innovation: changes one or more components without significantly affecting the overall system design. Architectural innovation: changes the overall system design or how components interact.Most architectural innovations also require component changes. Source: Henderson & Clark (1990).Example: Adding gel-filled material to a bicycle seat (component). Transition from high-wheel bicycle to safety bicycle (architectural).
According to Henderson and Clark (1990), what is the 'Innovation Matrix' and what are its four cells?	A 2x2 matrix combining architectural knowledge (reinforced vs. overturned) and component knowledge (reinforced vs. overturned), yielding: Incremental, Modular, Architectural, and Radical innovationThe matrix shows that established firms most struggle with architectural innovations — even when individual components are familiar, changes to how they interact can destroy existing organizational knowledge.Example: A radical innovation (e.g., transistor replacing vacuum tube) overturns both architectural and component knowledge. Source: Henderson, R. and Clark, K. (1990), Administrative Science Quarterly.
What are 'sustaining' versus 'disruptive' technologies, and which is harder for established firms to adapt to?	Sustaining technologies augment existing performance attributes; disruptive technologies incorporate different performance attributes — disruptive technologies are harder for established firms to adapt toEstablished firms optimize for their existing customers' needs, making them systematically blind to technologies that serve different performance dimensions or new customer segments.Example: Digital photography was disruptive to Kodak (different performance attributes — no film, instant review) but more sustaining for Sony (leveraged existing electronics competencies).
What is 'organizational ambidexterity' and why do firms need it?	Organizational ambidexterity is the ability to simultaneously exploit existing resources/capabilities (for today) AND explore new opportunities (for tomorrow)Firms face a paradox: exploitation generates current income but can crowd out exploration; exploration builds future competencies but costs resources today. Without ambidexterity, firms may succeed short-term but fail long-term.Example: Amazon exploits its retail and cloud businesses while exploring new areas like autonomous delivery and healthcare.
What is the difference between 'structural ambidexterity' and 'contextual ambidexterity'?	Structural ambidexterity: exploration and exploitation allocated to separate organizational units. Contextual ambidexterity: the same units and people perform both exploration and exploitation.Structural ambidexterity reduces internal conflict but can create integration challenges. Contextual ambidexterity is more flexible but requires employees capable of switching between modes.Example: Structural — Lockheed's 'Skunk Works' (separate exploratory unit). Contextual — Google's 20% time policy (same employees explore and exploit).
What are the three 'modes of balancing' exploration and exploitation listed in the antecedents framework?	1) Contextual, 2) Structural, 3) TemporalTemporal balancing means switching between exploration and exploitation phases over time rather than doing both simultaneously — for example, a firm may have distinct innovation cycles and efficiency cycles.Example: A firm may pursue heavy exploration during a technological discontinuity, then switch to exploitation once a dominant design emerges.
What is 'appropriability' in the context of innovation, and what two factors determine it?	Appropriability is the degree to which a firm can capture the rents (profits) from its innovation. It is determined by: 1) how easily/quickly competitors can copy the innovation, and 2) the legal and strategic protections the firm employs.High appropriability means the innovator captures most of the value; low appropriability means value leaks to imitators, customers, or complementors.Example: Pharmaceutical companies use patents to achieve high appropriability for drug innovations.
What are the four main forms of legal intellectual property protection, and what does each cover?	1) Patents — exclusive rights to a new product, process, substance, or design. 2) Copyrights — exclusive rights to artistic, dramatic, and musical works. 3) Trademarks — exclusive rights to words, symbols, or marks distinguishing goods/services. 4) Trade Secrets — protection of chemical formulae, recipes, and industrial processes.Employment contracts restricting knowledge transfer are an additional protective mechanism.Example: Coca-Cola protects its formula as a trade secret rather than a patent (to avoid disclosure requirements).
Why might a firm strategically choose NOT to protect its innovation through patents?	Not protecting can encourage others to support the technology, increasing its likelihood of becoming the dominant designIf network effects and installed base are more valuable than exclusivity, open access can be strategically superior to protection. Licensing or open standards can build a larger ecosystem faster.Example: Google made Android open-source to maximize adoption and build a large installed base, rather than protecting it exclusively.
What are the four key antecedents of the 'appropriability regime' that affect how much value an innovator can capture?	1) Legal protection, 2) Complementary resources, 3) Imitability of the technology, 4) Lead timeThese factors together shape the competitive dynamics around an innovation — a strong appropriability regime means innovators can profit; a weak one means imitators or complementors capture more value.Example: EMI invented the CT scanner but lacked complementary resources (distribution, service networks) to appropriate the value — GE eventually dominated the market.
What is a 'modular system' and when is modularity most valuable?	A modular system can be separated and recombined to change configuration, scale, or functions via standardized interfaces. Modularity is most valuable when: a) there are diverse technological options to recombine, and b) customers have heterogeneous preferences.Modularity enables components from different producers to be combined (open modularity) or from a single firm (closed modularity).Example: Smartphones (open modularity — apps from many developers). IKEA shelving systems (closed modularity — only IKEA components recombined).
What defines a 'platform ecosystem,' and why do all members' success depend on each other?	A platform ecosystem is one where a core product/platform mediates relationships between a wide range of complements/components and end-users. All members' success depends on the success of other members because value is co-created across the ecosystem.Members often invest in co-specialization or exclusivity agreements. Platform boundaries can be stable or dynamic. Source: Van Alstyne, Parker & Choudary (2016), Harvard Business Review.Example: A video game console (platform) connects game developers (complementors) and gamers (users) — if the platform fails, both developers and users lose their investments.
